articles.title: Utah home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Utah and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for Utah and why an index cannot value a specific property.

sections.heading: Evidence check: what changed over the quarter and year
body: Start with the dated local finding: As of 2026 Q1, Utah's purchase-only HPI rose 0.8% over the latest quarter and fell 0.0% over the year. In practical terms, the quarterly window is more sensitive to recent movement, while the annual window compares the latest quarter with the matching quarter one year earlier. That difference may matter when a borrower checks whether search, offer, or refinance assumptions still fit the broad direction of the state market. For the decision at hand, if the two windows diverge, timing is part of the answer; if they align, local variation still remains. Neither change identifies a home's value, a competitive offer, an affordable payment, or what the index will do next, so current property and financing evidence must carry the decision.

sections.heading: Evidence check: place the latest movement in a longer sequence
body: Over the five years ending 2026 Q1, the Utah HPI rose 32.9%, compared with the latest annual movement of 0.0%. The longer window shows how far the repeat-sale index moved across several market cycles, while the one-year figure gives more weight to recent conditions. That contrast may help a borrower recognize when an older search budget, equity assumption, or comparable sale needs fresh evidence. For borrower planning, it does not justify applying the five-year percentage to an old purchase price, and it cannot forecast a future sale. Even when the long and short directions agree, current comparable properties, taxes, insurance, financing costs, condition, and a property-specific payment worksheet remain necessary before the trend informs a mortgage decision.

sections.heading: Borrower view: use the index to test local assumptions
body: A Utah search plan can apply the quarterly, annual, and five-year windows to ask whether current listings, seller concessions, and property types resemble the broad state movement. At an annual movement of 0.0%, the dated 723.3 index level provides a reference point for that question without supplying a sale price. If the windows point in different directions, the divergence shows why the selected period matters; if they point together, statewide consistency still does not erase local variation. For the decision at hand, the plan therefore should compare the broad pattern with current listings and recent property-level evidence rather than treating any percentage as a forecast. FHFA HPI measures repeat-sale movement, not the complete set of homes available to one borrower.

sections.heading: Start with the evidence: the index level is context, not a home price
body: Utah's latest FHFA purchase-only HPI level is 723.3.

Applied to a decision in Utah, that number is a benchmark for repeat-sale price movement, not a dollar-denominated typical home price, listing price, or appraisal. Reading the 723.3 level with the 0.8% quarterly change keeps the index itself separate from its rate of movement. For the decision at hand, a borrower can apply the dated level to identify the release being discussed, but not to value a selected property. Local inventory, property type, condition, concessions, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association costs, and financing terms still need current evidence. This boundary is especially important when broad statewide movement differs from the neighborhood, price band, or property type involved in an actual purchase, refinance, or equity review.

sections.heading: The HPI cannot value a property or set a payment
body: An HPI level of 723.3 does not establish the dollar value of a property in Utah, and an annual movement of 0.0% does not establish how much any home gained or lost. For the decision at hand, the index excludes the borrower's down payment, loan amount, interest rate, taxes, insurance, association costs, repairs, and time horizon. FHFA can also revise published values, so the 0.8% and 32.9% comparisons should retain their source period. In practical terms, the 723.3 level must remain separate from the dollar figures used in a property review. Those limits make the state index useful for orientation but unsuitable as an appraisal, offer recommendation, equity calculation, promised appreciation claim, payment quote, or underwriting conclusion.

sections.heading: Connect the state trend to current property evidence
body: A practical Utah borrower review can record 0.8%, 0.0%, and 32.9% as statewide trend markers, then compare them with current local listings and recent property-level evidence. For a purchase, keep the 0.8% state movement as context while adding the selected price, inspection findings, appraisal process, taxes, insurance, cash-to-close, and loan disclosures.

Applied to a decision in Utah, for a refinance or equity question, begin with a defensible property review instead of multiplying a prior value by 0.0% or 32.9%. For the decision at hand, this approach preserves the information in the 723.3 HPI while giving current local and borrower-specific facts the decision-making role that a statewide repeat-sale index cannot fill.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Utah statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for Utah.
articles.previewText: See what changed in Utah's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: See what changed in Utah's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
keyTakeaways: change in unemployment rate from the prior month: -0.1 percentage points.
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier: 0.2 percentage points.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: Comparison view: what changed in the latest month
body: Start with the dated local finding: As of May 2026, Utah's unemployment rate fell from 3.8% in April 2026 to 3.7% in May 2026, a change of 0.1 percentage points. That movement may matter when a borrower is pressure-testing timing, reserves, or the documentation needed after a job or income change. It cannot show that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it cannot prove one applicant's income stability, mortgage eligibility, or loan price. For borrower planning, the release also estimates 67,863 people unemployed within a labor force of 1,825,206, which shows the scale of the movement without turning a state-level statistic into a borrower-level conclusion. Because LAUS values can be revised, keep the May 2026 source period attached to the comparison and verify household records separately.

sections.heading: Comparison view: what changed over the year
body: Over the year ending May 2026, Utah's unemployment rate rose from 3.5% in May 2025 to 3.7%, a change of 0.2 percentage points.

That wider window helps show whether Utah's latest monthly movement sits inside a longer pattern, while the one-month movement of 0.1 percentage points answers a shorter timing question. Neither comparison predicts the next labor-market release for Utah or describes the stability of one household's earnings. For mortgage planning in Utah, the practical consequence is to date broad economic assumptions and then return to current pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, assets, and debts. Those borrower records carry more weight in a loan review than movement in Utah's broad labor-market estimates.

sections.heading: Evidence at a glance: the counts behind the published rate
body: For the May 2026 source period, BLS estimated 1,825,206 people in the Utah labor force, including 1,757,343 employed and 67,863 unemployed under LAUS definitions. In practical terms, the employment and unemployment counts belong together because they establish the scale behind the published 3.7% rate. For mortgage planning, that state-level labor force describes the surrounding economy rather than the income or employment record of an applicant. For the decision at hand, it can nevertheless help a borrower understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, property facts, and the rules of the loan being considered. The counts should, as a result, follow the change summary instead of replacing the borrower answer.

sections.heading: Why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 3.7%, Utah's unemployment rate summarizes 67,863 unemployed people within a labor force of 1,825,206; the rate should not be discussed without those counts.

In a borrower review centered on Utah, the 1,757,343 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 1,757,343 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. Keeping the 3.7% rate attached to 1,825,206, 1,757,343, and 67,863 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Keep the boundary clear: labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 1,757,343 employed people nor an unemployment rate of 3.7% tells a lender what an applicant in Utah earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue. The monthly movement of 0.1 percentage points also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 3.7% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. For borrower planning, that boundary protects borrowers from treating the year-over-year movement of 0.2 percentage points in either direction as an approval signal or as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Next step: turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A useful next step for borrowers in Utah is to note the latest 3.7% unemployment rate and the year-over-year movement of 0.2 percentage points, then move immediately to the records that describe the household. After noting the 3.7% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path.

The 1,825,206 local labor force and 1,757,343 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. The monthly movement of 0.1 percentage points remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping Utah's 3.7% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS state monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Utah housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Utah with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for Utah with survey limitations and practical questions.
keyTakeaways: As of: 2024 ACS 5-year estimates. Source period: 2024.
prior ACS state median home value: $279,100.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

sections.heading: The dated finding: statewide value, income, and owner costs
body: The published evidence provides the starting point: Utah's 2024 ACS housing baseline reports a median owner-occupied home value of $489,400, median household income of $95,166, and median selected monthly owner costs of $2,009 for owners with a mortgage. For the decision at hand, those three reference points may help a borrower identify which statewide value, income, and recurring-cost assumptions need local replacement first. They remain separate medians, not the price, income, and monthly budget of one typical household, and the $2,009 measure is not a real-estate-tax figure or a current mortgage quote. In practical terms, a real decision continues to require local property evidence, borrower documentation, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and current loan information.

sections.heading: Start with the evidence: keep owner costs and rent comparable
body: The same Utah release reports $1,496 in median gross rent alongside $2,009 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage. In practical terms, the two measures cover different housing populations and cost definitions, so $1,496 should not be compared with only principal and interest or used to declare a statewide break-even point. A useful comparison replaces the $1,496 and $2,009 medians with a current lease and a current property budget, including utilities, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, transaction costs, and the expected time horizon. For the decision at hand, the $95,166 statewide median can provide economic context, but dividing it by either $1,496 or $2,009 would still combine medians that do not represent the same household.

sections.heading: Planning context: vacancy is broader than homes for sale
body: Utah's vacant-unit share is 8.1% within a statewide stock of 1,223,468 housing units. In practical terms, ACS vacancy includes categories beyond property currently offered for sale or rent, so 8.1% is not a live inventory rate and cannot measure competition for a selected home.

Applied to a decision in Utah, read beside the 70.2% owner share and 29.8% renter share, the vacancy figure helps describe how the broad stock was classified without predicting near-term supply. For the decision at hand, a borrower should use current listings, property condition, days on market, concessions, and local professional context to answer transaction questions that 8.1% and 1,223,468 were not designed to resolve.

sections.heading: Borrower view: how occupied housing is divided by tenure
body: Across Utah's measured housing stock of 1,223,468 units, the owner share of occupied housing is 70.2% and the renter share is 29.8%, making owner-occupied housing the larger statewide tenure group. For the decision at hand, the 70.2% and 29.8% shares describe how occupied units were distributed during the survey period; they do not measure current buyer demand, owner willingness to sell, or renter readiness to purchase.

The 70.2% and 29.8% statewide tenure split is useful for understanding scale and checking assumptions about the housing mix. In practical terms, it remains too broad to select a product or characterize a particular city, county, property type, or household inside Utah.

sections.heading: Keep the statewide ACS vintages separate
body: The 2019 ACS published $279,100 as Utah's median owner-occupied home-value estimate, while the 2024 ACS published $489,400.

For the next comparison in Utah, these nominal statewide observations may still help show why an older planning reference needs current local evidence. They are not inflation-adjusted, and the ACS home-value methodology changed between the vintages, so the two dollar figures are not a like-for-like appreciation series and should not be converted into a precise percentage change. For borrower planning, both values summarize multi-year survey windows and carry sampling error. Keep $279,100 attached to 2019 and $489,400 attached to 2024, then use current local and property-specific evidence for purchase, refinance, or equity assumptions.

sections.heading: Borrower action: move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: A borrower may carry $489,400, $2,009, $1,496, and the 8.1% vacancy share into a local comparison as dated statewide context, then replace each with evidence matched to the actual decision. For ownership, replace the $489,400 and $2,009 medians with a selected price, down payment, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and cash reserves. For the rental budget, replace the $1,496 median with a comparable lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes over the same horizon. In practical terms, the 29.8% renter share is useful for describing the statewide tenure mix, not for predicting which local option will be available. The 70.2% tenure measure may help explain the statewide housing mix, but it cannot decide whether buying, renting, refinancing, or using equity is suitable for one Utah household.

data.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

rows: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a separate 2019 nominal home-value observation. It does not calculate a percentage change between home-value vintages. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The 2019 and 2024 home-value dollars are not inflation-adjusted, and their methodology is not directly comparable. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: Utah 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across Utah counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Utah without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across Utah without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 29.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The conforming range across county records
body: Utah's statewide summary covers 29 county or county-equivalent records. In practical terms, within those records, the 2026 one-unit conforming limit runs from $832,750 to $1,150,000. That range may help borrowers see why the property county must be identified before a loan amount is assigned a conforming or jumbo category. For the decision at hand, the $832,750 to $1,150,000 range confirms that one conforming ceiling does not apply uniformly across Utah. The 29 record count keeps the range tied to the statewide source coverage instead of an assumed national rule. The $832,750 to $1,150,000 amounts classify loan size within the published program table; they do not establish a borrower's capacity, a property's value, or the price of financing. Before using the $1,150,000 statewide maximum, county, legal unit count, and expected base loan amount must be known.

sections.heading: Evidence at a glance: the FHA range follows its own county table
body: Across the same 29 Utah records, the 2026 one-unit FHA limit spans $541,287 to $1,163,800. For borrower planning, the $541,287 low and $1,163,800 high show that FHA's published boundary also varies within the state.

In a borrower review centered on Utah, the $541,287 floor and $1,163,800 high remain county-table values even when they match conforming amounts elsewhere. The FHA range should not be merged with the $832,750 to $1,150,000 conforming range, because the programs use different authorities and requirements. A $541,287 to $1,163,800 comparison can route loan-amount and product-category questions, but it cannot decide FHA eligibility, mortgage insurance, appraisal acceptability, occupancy compliance, cash needs, or approval.

sections.heading: Comparison view: conforming and FHA ranges answer different questions
body: The highest one-unit conforming amount in Utah is $1,150,000, while the highest FHA amount is $1,163,800; at the low end, the figures are $832,750 and $541,287. For borrower planning, these pairs show program boundaries, not competing offers.

In a borrower review centered on Utah, a requested amount below $1,150,000 may still fall outside the applicable county's conforming limit, and an amount below $1,163,800 may still face FHA borrower and property requirements. Comparing $832,750, $1,150,000, $541,287, and $1,163,800 is useful only when county and unit count are held constant. The $1,150,000 and $1,163,800 ceilings leave rates, fees, mortgage insurance, appraisal, reserves, debt capacity, and product suitability as separate questions for the documented transaction.

sections.heading: Start with the evidence: why county verification matters
body: 4 Utah counties sit above the conforming baseline in this summary, while the full conforming range reaches from $832,750 to $1,150,000. In practical terms, that count shows where a statewide shortcut can fail: a property in a higher-limit county may use a different published ceiling than one at $832,750. The 4 figure does not mean those counties are more affordable, more desirable, or more likely to approve a loan.

For the decision at hand, it only describes how many matched county rows exceed the baseline. The $1,150,000 maximum should therefore be treated as evidence that county lookup matters, not as the default Utah limit. Before treating $1,150,000 as relevant, a borrower should identify the property's official county or county-equivalent area and then attach conventional, jumbo, or FHA labels to the desired loan amount.

sections.heading: Evidence limits: a larger county limit is not greater affordability
body: A $1,150,000 conforming ceiling or $1,163,800 FHA ceiling does not make that loan amount affordable to a household in Utah, and the lower $832,750 or $541,287 figures do not describe home prices. Published $1,150,000 and $1,163,800 limits cannot establish borrower income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, property condition, appraisal, insurance, reserves, rates, fees, or final approval. The $1,150,000 and $1,163,800 figures can also change with a new calendar year or corrected property geography. For borrower planning, keeping the 29 county-record scope and 2026 period attached to the figures prevents the statewide summary from being mistaken for a personalized product recommendation or a single amount that applies everywhere.

sections.heading: Put the evidence to work: use the range to reach the correct county row
body: A useful next step is to verify the selected property's Utah county or county-equivalent area, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. In practical terms, then compare that amount with the exact official row, using the $832,750 to $1,150,000 conforming range and $541,287 to $1,163,800 FHA range only as orientation.

Applied to a decision in Utah, the 4 above-baseline count explains why this lookup matters, but it does not replace it. For the decision at hand, keep the selected county row with the worksheet so the $1,150,000 and $1,163,800 statewide maximums cannot be mistaken for property-specific figures. After the 4 count prompts that county lookup, a licensed professional can explain current limits and borrower, property, mortgage-insurance, and documentation rules without presenting the largest statewide figure as available borrowing power.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
